inline_review:
commit 82c1916e72710143e253e2a09044487148f0c4e8
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>

mm/damon: introduce DAMON-based NUMA memory tiering module

Adds a new DAMON special-purpose module for NUMA memory tiering. It monitors
physical memory access patterns and migrates hot pages from slow NUMA nodes
to fast NUMA nodes (promotion), and cold pages in the opposite direction
(demotion).

> diff --git a/mm/damon/tier.c b/mm/damon/tier.c
> new file mode 100644
> index 0000000000000..4a5078685f1f7
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/mm/damon/tier.c

[ ... ]

> +/*
> + * NUMA node ID of the fast (promote target) memory tier.
> + *
> + * Pages that are hot on the slow node will be migrated to this node.
> + * Cold pages on this node will be demoted to the slow node.  0 by default.
> + */
> +static int promote_target_nid __read_mostly;
> +module_param(promote_target_nid, int, 0600);
> +
> +/*
> + * NUMA node ID of the slow (demote target) memory tier.
> + *
> + * Pages that are cold on the fast node will be migrated to this node.
> + * Hot pages on this node will be promoted to the fast node.  1 by default.
> + */
> +static int demote_target_nid __read_mostly = 1;
> +module_param(demote_target_nid, int, 0600);

Are there bounds checks to prevent out-of-bounds memory accesses if an
invalid NUMA node ID is written to these parameters?

Passing a negative value or one exceeding MAX_NUMNODES-1 to si_meminfo_node()
could cause an out-of-bounds access on the global node_data array.

[ ... ]

> +static int damon_tier_add_filters(struct damos *promote_scheme,
> +		struct damos *demote_scheme)
> +{
> +	struct damos_filter *filter;
> +
> +	/* skip promoting pages that are already young (recently accessed) */
> +	filter = damos_new_filter(DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG, true, true);

This isn't a bug, but the comment seems to contradict the code.

The filter uses allow=true, which explicitly allows matching (young) pages
and implicitly rejects non-matching pages.

Should the comment be updated to say it *only* promotes young pages?

[ ... ]

> +static int damon_tier_apply_parameters(void)
> +{

[ ... ]

> +	err = damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default(param_target,
> +					&monitor_region_start,
> +					&monitor_region_end,
> +					param_ctx->min_region_sz);

Is this call missing the addr_unit argument? The function signature expects
five arguments, but only four are provided here.

Also, will damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default() restrict monitoring
to just a single NUMA node?

If it only monitors the largest System RAM resource (usually the slow tier),
how will the demotion scheme observe accesses on the fast node to identify
cold pages for demotion?

[ ... ]

> +static int damon_tier_enabled_store(const char *val,
> +		const struct kernel_param *kp)
> +{
> +	bool is_enabled = enabled;
> +	bool enable;
> +	int err;
> +
> +	err = kstrtobool(val, &enable);
> +	if (err)
> +		return err;
> +
> +	if (is_enabled == enable)
> +		return 0;
> +
> +	/* Called before init function.  The function will handle this. */
> +	if (!damon_initialized())
> +		goto set_param_out;
> +
> +	err = damon_tier_turn(enable);

If damon_modules_new_paddr_ctx_target() fails during module initialization
(e.g., due to ENOMEM), the global ctx pointer remains NULL.

Because built-in module parameters are registered early, could a user later
write 'Y' to /sys/module/damon_tier/parameters/enabled, bypass the
damon_initialized() check, and cause a NULL pointer dereference in
damon_commit_ctx(ctx, param_ctx) when damon_tier_turn() is called?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Missing argument in damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default() call.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"In `damon_tier_apply_parameters()`, the call to `damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default()` provides only 4 arguments (`param_target`, `&monitor_region_start`, `&monitor_region_end`, and `param_ctx->min_region_sz`). The function signature requires 5 arguments, including `addr_unit`. The omission of `param_ctx->addr_unit` will cause a build failure."},{"problem":"Default monitoring region restricts monitoring to a single NUMA node, breaking bidirectional tiering.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"By default, `damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default()` configures the monitoring target to a single contiguous physical memory region (the largest 'System RAM' resource). In a NUMA architecture, this typically corresponds to the memory of a single node (usually the slow tier). Because the fast tier is entirely unmonitored by default, the demotion scheme will never observe accesses on the fast node and will fail to demote any cold pages. This architectural flaw breaks the core bidirectional migration capability of the module."},{"problem":"Missing bounds check on NUMA node ID parameters allows out-of-bounds memory access.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"The module parameters `promote_target_nid` and `demote_target_nid` lack bounds validation. These unvalidated integers are assigned to `goal->nid` and passed to `si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid)` via `damos_get_node_mem_bp()`. Inside `si_meminfo_node()`, the macro `NODE_DATA(nid)` is used to index into the global `node_data` array. Because these parameters are writable by privileged users at runtime via sysfs, passing a negative value or a value exceeding `MAX_NUMNODES-1` causes an out-of-bounds memory access, leading to a kernel panic."},{"problem":"Initialization failure leaves global context NULL, leading to a kernel panic upon sysfs write.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"If `damon_modules_new_paddr_ctx_target(&ctx, &target)` fails during module initialization (e.g., due to ENOMEM), the global `ctx` pointer remains `NULL`. Because built-in module parameters are registered early and persist even if the initcall fails, userspace can subsequently write 'Y' to `/sys/module/damon_tier/parameters/enabled`. This invokes `damon_tier_enabled_store()`, which bypasses the `damon_initialized()` check (as the core is ready) and calls `damon_tier_apply_parameters() -> damon_commit_ctx(ctx, param_ctx)`. `damon_commit_ctx()` immediately dereferences `dst->maybe_corrupted`, leading to a definitive NULL pointer dereference and system crash. Note: This structural issue is copied from other DAMON modules, but is newly introduced into `damon_tier.c`."},{"problem":"Contradictory behavior between the promote_scheme's YOUNG filter descriptive comment and its implementation.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"The comment above the `promote_scheme` filter states 'skip promoting pages that are already young'. However, the code initializes the filter with `damos_new_filter(DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG, true, true)`. In DAMON, a final filter with `allow=true` explicitly allows matching pages (young) and implicitly rejects non-matching pages (not young) due to the `damos_filters_default_reject()` evaluation. The code functions correctly by promoting *only* hot/young pages, but the descriptive comment incorrectly claims the exact opposite."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 82c1916e72710143e253e2a09044487148f0c4e8
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>

Adds a new DAMON special-purpose module for NUMA memory tiering. It monitors
